one_line_takeaway: PersonaMark watermarks LLM output by biasing sentence-structure hash bits keyed to each user's ID, giving vocabulary-independent, synonym-attack-robust (AUC 0.97 at 40% substitution) per-user attribution with far lower text-quality damage than token-level KGW (perplexity 2.74 vs 12.32 on Gemma-2B).
key_finding: PersonaMark preserves text quality far better than the KGW baseline while retaining strong, robust, attributable detection. On Gemma-2B-IT it achieves perplexity 2.74 vs KGW's 12.32 (un-watermarked baseline 8.83), and across all four models its sentiment, alignment, and readability deltas from the un-watermarked model are the smallest. Detection AUC reaches ~1.00 and stays robust under attack: on Phi-3.5 the AUC is still 0.97 under a 40% synonym-substitution attack (0.98 at 30%, 0.99 at 20%, 1.00 at 10%), because sentence structure is largely invariant to synonym swaps. KGW's personalized variant (KGW-Persona) degrades badly on small-vocabulary models (Phi-3.5 AUC drops to 0.86 and the model even fails to follow prompts), whereas PersonaMark's sentence-structure space is vocabulary-independent. Attribution is scalable: hashing all users' functions to identify the author takes 3.86 s for 100,000 users (linear above ~1000 users).
core_contribution: PersonaMark is a plug-in LLM text-watermarking scheme that embeds a per-user watermark by controlling sentence STRUCTURE rather than token/logit distributions. For each generation step it produces K candidate sentences via sentence-oriented diverse beam search, extracts each candidate's dependency-parse structure into a string, hashes that string (BLAKE2, mod 2) into a binary bit, and keeps only bit-1 candidates so the output paragraph is statistically biased toward bit-1 sentences (detectable via a Z-test). Personalization is achieved by concatenating the logged-in user ID into the hash input, giving each user a distinct partition of the sentence-structure space; at detection, whichever user's hash function makes the text pass the Z-test reveals the responsible user ID. The authors claim this is the first study of PERSONALIZED (per-user attribution) LLM watermarking. Note: KGW, SWEET, DiPmark, MPAC, SemStamp etc. are cited RELATED WORK / baselines, not this paper's contribution; the sentence-structure carrier + user-ID hashing for attribution is what is novel here.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section 3.1. Defender goal: embed a human-invisible, per-user watermark into generated text while preserving model behavior; defender has WHITE-BOX access to the model (weights and generation strategy). Adversary goal: erase the watermark while preserving the content's unique language/persona style; adversary has only BLACK-BOX API access through a limited number of user accounts (user-control systems restrict how many samples can be collected). Adversary may sample outputs, infer the watermarking rule, and edit texts (e.g., synonym substitution) as long as they stay functional and stylistically intact. Use case: tracing leaked/misused output of a proprietary personalized LLM back to a specific user or employee for accountability.
limitations: Author-stated (Section 7.1): (1) watermark injection uses beam search, so it costs more compute and per-user inference time than normal generation; (2) it depends on sentence structure conveying stylistic information, so it may be weak where structure is not stylistically meaningful; (3) it operates at the sentence level and is not applicable to single-sentence outputs (mitigated by the fact that typical requests are paragraph-length). Reviewer-noted gaps: robustness is only reported against synonym substitution and a case-study paraphrase, not against strong paraphrase-based or LLM-rewrite attacks that DO alter sentence structure (the paper's own threat model assumes the adversary preserves style, which conveniently preserves structure); the attribution collision/false-attribution rate as the user base grows (whether two users' hash partitions could both pass the Z-test) is asserted to be handled by hashing uniqueness but not empirically bounded; and detection cost grows linearly with the number of users.

datasets_used: Base models evaluated: Gemma-2B-IT, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, OLMo-7B-Instruct, Phi-3.5-mini-Instruct
MGTBench used for the AI-generated-text detector comparison
quantitative_results: Gemma-2B-IT perplexity: PersonaMark 2.74 vs KGW 12.32 vs KGW-Persona 6.53 vs un-watermarked 8.83 (Table 1)
Mistral-7B perplexity: PersonaMark 2.10 vs KGW 3.81 vs un-watermarked 1.78
OLMo-7B perplexity: PersonaMark 2.39 vs KGW 7.54 vs un-watermarked 2.37
Phi-3.5 perplexity: PersonaMark 3.19 vs KGW 6.84 vs KGW-Persona 8.05 vs un-watermarked 2.10
Phi-3.5 detection AUC under synonym attack: 1.00 (0%), 1.00 (10%), 0.99 (20%), 0.98 (30%), 0.97 (40%); KGW ~0.96-0.97 across the same range
Phi-3.5 watermark AUC: PersonaMark 1.00, KGW 0.97, KGW-Persona 0.86 (Fig 4)
Alignment (key-info similarity) higher for PersonaMark on all models, e.g. Phi-3.5 0.59 vs KGW-Persona 0.11
Human win-rate averages: fluency 88.75%, content consistency 77.75% (Phi-3.5 100%/100%, Mistral 100%/85%) vs KGW-Persona (Table 2)
Case study Z-scores (Gemma): un-watermarked Z=2.694 p=0.004, PersonaMark Z=5.000 p=2.86e-7, perturbed PersonaMark Z=3.800 p=7.23e-5
Phi-3.5 residual vocabulary after KGW-Persona: 16,032 of 32,064 tokens (half), cited as cause of its generation failure
baselines_compared: KGW (Kirchenbauer et al., green/red-list logit watermark) as primary quality/detection baseline
KGW-Persona (authors' modified KGW with a frozen per-user green list) as the multi-user attribution baseline
Un-watermarked model outputs as the behavior-preservation reference
Seven AI-generated-text detectors from MGTBench (likelihood, rank, log_rank, entropy, rank_GLTR, RoBERTa detector) for the zero-bit detection comparison
